coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive colorimetric detection of glucose through glucose oxidase and Cu2+-catalyzed 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine oxidation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xin
Gao, Linna
Chen, Zhengbo
coredata.dc:description: We develop a glucose oxidase (GOx)-mediated strategy for detecting glucose based on oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (oxTMB), which is generated from Cu2+-catalyzed 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB)-H2O2 reaction, as colorimetric readout. The sensing system involves two processes: generation of H2O2 from GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose, and H2O2-induced the oxidization of TMB via the catalysis of Cu2+. The H2O2 formed by GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose oxidizes colorless TMB to blue oxTMB, thus enhancing the absorbance intensity at 670 nm. Therefore, we draw a conclusion that the enhancement in colorimetric signal relies directly on H2O2 concentration, which, in turn, depends on glucose concentration. This color change can be used not only for visual detection of glucose by naked eyes but for reliable glucose quantification in the range from 1 to 100 nM with a detection limit of 0.21 nM. The method possesses the following advantages: simple design, low experimental cost, and no any additional experimental equipment for heating, illuminating, or bubbling.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gluocose
Glucose oxidase
Colorimetric
3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine
Hydrogen peroxide
Copper ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design and Establishment of Glucose Sensing System

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Dependence of the absorbance of the sensing solution on (A) Cu2+ concentration, (B) GOx concentration, (C) pH of PBS, (D) pH of HAc-NaAc solution, (E) TMB concentration, and (F) incubation time.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and (B) photograph of the sensing solution against different concentrations of glucose: 0, 1, 5, 10, 50, and 100 nM. (C) Plot of the absorbance versus different concentrations of glucose. Inset: a linear relationship with the logarithmic value of glucose concentration in the range from 1 to 100 nM. (D) The calculation of the LOD. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. TMB concentration: 15 mM, GOX concentration: 100 nM, Cu2+ concentration: 40 mM, and incubation time: 60 min.
Photograph and the absorbance change of the sensing system in the presence of interfering substances (0.1 mM), the mixture of interfering substances (0.1 mM), and glucose (10 μM).
Schematic illustration of colorimetric glucose sensor by utilizing GOx and Cu2+-catalyzed TMB oxidation.
Comparison of the performances of sensing strategies for glucose.
Determination results of glucose in human serum sample (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive colorimetric detection of glucose through glucose oxidase and Cu2+-catalyzed 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine oxidation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing 100048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Shandong University of Science and Technology, Qingdao 266590, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Environmental Engineering
Shandong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We develop a glucose oxidase (GOx)-mediated strategy for detecting glucose based on oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (oxTMB), which is generated from Cu2+-catalyzed 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB)-H2O2 reaction, as colorimetric readout. The sensing system involves two processes: generation of H2O2 from GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose, and H2O2-induced the oxidization of TMB via the catalysis of Cu2+. The H2O2 formed by GOx-catalyzed oxidation of glucose oxidizes colorless TMB to blue oxTMB, thus enhancing the absorbance intensity at 670 nm. Therefore, we draw a conclusion that the enhancement in colorimetric signal relies directly on H2O2 concentration, which, in turn, depends on glucose concentration. This color change can be used not only for visual detection of glucose by naked eyes but for reliable glucose quantification in the range from 1 to 100 nM with a detection limit of 0.21 nM. The method possesses the following advantages: simple design, low experimental cost, and no any additional experimental equipment for heating, illuminating, or bubbling.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that blood glucose in a very high level is a warning of diabetes, which is one of the most common and lethiferous diseases [1,2]. According to the report from the World Health Organization, over 570 million people in the world were affected with diabetes in 2030 [3,4]. Although blood glucose levels in healthy individuals and people with diabetes are both in the mM range, the interferences of complicated blood matrix affect detection of glucose [5,6], resulting in the requirement for sample dilution procedures before the measurement. Hence, the effective and highly sensitive detection of the blood glucose level is very important for monitoring and controlling diabetes as well as avoiding serious health complications including cardiac disease, kidney failure, blindness, and high blood pressure [7–9].

In recent years, various methods for glucose detection have been reported, including electrochemistry [10,11], fluorescence [12–14], Raman scattering [15,16], chemiluminescence [17], mass spectrometry [18], and colorimetry [19]. Most of these approaches, while promising, suffer from some disadvantages such as expensive cost, operational complexity, and unsatisfactory detection limits. Among these methods, colorimetric sensors have sparked tremendous interest because of low cost, direct measurement, low background signal, and no need of any advanced instrumentation, facilitating the detection by the naked eye and UV–vis spectrometer [20–23].

In this work, we design H2O2-assisted glucose detection based on glucose oxidase (GOx)-catalyzed glucose-O2-H2O-reaction. GOx has been widely used in the optical detection of glucose via the enzymatic oxidation [19,24]. GOx catalyzes the oxidation of glucose to generate gluconic acid and H2O2. However, these experiments are costly and low sensitive. Colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) is oxidized to blue oxidized TMB (oxTMB) by H2O2 formed from the above enzymatic oxidation via the catalysis of Cu2+. The Cu2+-catalyzed TMB- H2O2 system not only possessed the properties of high sensitivity, but also showed some unique virtues such as convenience, high stability and low cost [25,26]. This results in increased absorbance intensity, allowing quantitative detection of glucose. The colorimetric method exhibits an ultrasensitive detection of glucose with the detection limit down to 0.21 nM. Compared with reported colorimetric glucose sensor based on the etching of Ag nanoprisms by H2O2 produced by GOx-O2-H2O system [19], our method has shown distinct advantages, such as lower detection limit, and no need to intervene with nanomaterials, which will not only save a lot of material, labor, and costs, but also help the practical application. The sensing strategy is universally applicable for detection of many other H2O2-mediated substrates and enzymes.

All reagents and chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), H2O2, glucose, glucose oxidase (GOx), fructose and uric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) was provided by a Millipore system (Millipore, USA) and used as a solvent in all experiments.
A Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Japan) was used to record the UV–vis spectra. Absorption spectra were recorded on at room temperature.

First, 60 μL of 50 nM GOx, and 60 μL of glucose standard solution with different concentrations (final concentrations: 0, 1, 5, 10, 50, and 100 nM) were mixed in 360 μL of 0.05 M PBS (pH 5.91) and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, 180 μL of 40 mM Cu2+ solution, 60 μL of 15 mM TMB, and 300 μL of 0.2 M HAc/NaAc buffer (pH 4.6) were successively added to the above solution. The mixed solution was incubated for another 30 min. Finally, the solution was transferred into 1 cm quartz cuvette and directly measured by UV–vis spectrometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and Establishment of Glucose Sensing System
ce:section.ce:para: The glucose sensing processes have been shown in Scheme 1 : firstly, glucose and GOx were thoroughly mixed, the reaction between glucose and oxygen and H2O was catalyzed by GOx to form gluconic acid. Then, the addition of Cu2+ and TMB induced that colorless TMB was oxidized by H2O2 generated from the enzymatic oxidation to blue oxTMB via the catalysis of Cu2+, the colorimetric changes can be facilely utilized to monitor the oxidation behaviors of TMB in the presence of different concentrations of glucose. The above reactions can be described by equations in Scheme 1.

In order to achieve the best performance for detecting target glucose, the experimental conditions, including Cu2+ concentration, GOx concentration, pH of buffer, TMB concentration, and incubation time of glucose, play crucial roles in the sensitivity of glucose detection and were investigated. The effect of Cu2+ concentration on the absorbance of oxidized TMB was investigated in the range of 0–60 mM. As shown in Fig. 1A, absorbance increased gradually with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 0–40 mM, and then decreased with further increasing Cu2+ concentration. At 40 mM, catalytic ability of Cu2+ was the best. Thus, the optimized concentration of Cu2+ for sensing system was 40 mM. Similarly, the effect of GOx concentration was studied in the range from 0 to 1000 nM (Fig. 1B). When GOx concentration was 100 nM, it achieved the best catalytic performance and the absorbance achieved the maximum. For GOx-catalyzed reaction between glucose and oxygen, and Cu2+-catalyzed TMB-H2O2 reaction, the effects of pH on catalytic activity of GOx and Cu2+ in the range of 5.29–7.17 and 3.6–5.4 were studied, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1(C, D), absorbance increased gradually with pH in the range of 5.29–5.91, and 3.6–4.6, and then decreased with further increasing pH value, which may be ascribed to the decreasing affinity of GOx and Cu2+ for aqueous phase. The effects of TMB concentration (0–25 mM) and incubation time (20–80 min) on absorbance were studied as shown in Fig. 1D and E. It was found that absorbance increased gradually with increasing TMB concentration and incubation time of glucose and then reached the maximum when TMB concentration was 15 mM and incubation time was 60 min. Thus, 15 mM and 60 min were chosen as the optimized TMB concentration and incubation time, respectively.

Under the optimal conditions, a series of samples containing different concentrations of glucose (final concentrations: 0, 1, 5, 10, 50, and 100 nM) have been tested. As shown in Fig. 2A, along with the increase of the glucose concentration, the absorbance peak at 670 nm was dramatically increased, accompanied by solution color changes from pale blue to blue (Fig. 2B). When glucose concentration was 100 nM, the absorbance value reached the maximum. That is, with the increased concentration of target glucose, the generated H2O2 increased correspondingly, and the more TMB was oxidized to oxTMB by H2O2. Accordingly, the absorbance peak change at 670 nm was employed to quantitatively scale the glucose concentration (Fig. 2C). In particular, there was a linear relationship between the absorbance change, ΔA = Ai-A0 (where Ai and A0 denote the absorbance peak at 670 nm obtained before and after incubation with different concentrations of glucose), and the logarithmic value of the concentration of glucose (1–100 nM), shown in inset of Fig. 2C. The sensing strategy offers excellent reproducibility (RSDs < 5.96%, n = 3). Moreover, a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.2097 nM was achieved based on the reported computing method (Fig. 2D) [27]. A comparison of analytical parameters between the proposed colorimetric method and previously reported methods was summarized in Table 1 . The LOD and linear range of glucose in this work were better than those of reported methods.

Selectivity of the assay was also explored. These potential interfering substances (fructose and uric acid) and the mixture were separately added to the above reaction solution. As shown in Fig. 3 , the absorbance signals show that at 0.1 mM concentration, none of these interfering substances interfered with the assay. In addition, the mixture of the two interfering substances also did not show any noticeable interference. The color changes of the solutions also confirm that these interfering substances did not interfere with the assay. The glucose solution (10 μM) showed blue color, whereas all other samples showed pale blue color, indicating that the colorimetric sensor responded specifically toward glucose.

The practical applicability of the colorimetric method was tested on the assay of glucose detection in human serum from our lab. Because the normal glucose level in healthy human blood is 3.9–6.1 mM, a sufficient dilution can not only effectively eliminate the potential interference in the assay, because the compositions of blood are very complex, which contains various peptides, proteins, amino acids, carbohydrate, etc., but also guarantee glucose concentration to be in the linear range of this proposed method. The result is shown in Table 2 , the recovery rates range from 93.74% to 95.22%, indicating the practicability of the proposed sensing platform.

In summary, we developed a simple but effective colorimetric sensing strategy to determine glucose by taking advantage of the GOx-glucose enzymatic oxidation mechanism and Cu2+-catalyzedTMB-H2O2 sensing system. Under optimal conditions, the colorimetric assay exhibited excellent sensitivity and good specificity for detecting glucose with a LOD of 0.21 nM by the naked eye. The LOD obtained was 4 orders of magnitude lower than those (μM) of conventional TMB-based colorimetric methods. In addition, the proposed method also showed great potential for glucose detection in urine and serum samples with the same LOD of 0.25 nM. The approach can be extended to other reactions involving the generation of H2O2 and offers a new idea for developing low cost and sensitive colorimetric sensors for potential application in medical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of Scientific Research Project of Beijing Educational Committee (Grant No. KM201710028009), Youth Innovative Research Team of Capital Normal University, and Capacity Building for Sci-Tech Innovation-Fundamental Scientific Research Funds (025185305000/195).
